text: The video shows an XI ETAP incisional hernia repair with complete posterior sheath closure. The surgical ports are placed in a standard fashion, medial to the left linea semilunaris, spaced approximately six centimeters apart.

The surgeon incises the posterior sheath approximately five millimeters lateral to the linea alba, entering the preperitoneal space. The hernia sac is being taken down, with the thin peritoneum visibly shredding during the reduction process.

The surgeon performs a crossover maneuver to the contralateral retrorectus space, incising the posterior sheath slightly lateral to the linea alba. The dissection continues through the areolar tissue within the retrorectus space to create space for mesh overlap.

The surgeon dissects in the right lower quadrant near the arcuate line, where the diastasis has disappeared and the linea alba appears narrow. Space is created for mesh overlap below the umbilicus. Below the arcuate line, the medial edge of the posterior sheath is closely approximated caudally, while cephalad, the separation widens, revealing several large defects in the peritoneum.

The surgeon incises the posterior sheath to expand the retrorectus space towards the xiphoid, creating additional space for mesh overlap. A piece of mesh is visible in the preperitoneal space, indicating a previous Morgagni hernia repair.

The surgeon completes the dissection, having mobilized and created space for mesh placement. They measure the defect sizes, noting two larger defects: an umbilical hernia and several smaller defects within the diastasis. The diastasis measures six centimeters wide, with the largest defect being four and a half centimeters wide. The total length of all defects is approximately nine centimeters, and the diastasis length intended for re-approximation is about fifteen centimeters.

The surgeon uses an 18-inch OV lock suture to re-approximate the diastasis and close the hernia defect. The sutures are placed sequentially without immediate tightening to prevent losing the ipsilateral edge of the linea alba. The surgeon then tightens the sutures in a pulley-like fashion to evenly distribute tension across the defect.

The surgeon tightens the sutures, bringing the defect edges together. The defect visibly closes as the sutures are tightened. The surgeon then runs each suture back on itself, carefully grabbing the edges of the posterior sheath, which are about five to ten millimeters wide.

The surgeon examines the peritoneal holes and assesses the possibility of closing the posterior sheath. Using a 2-0 V-lock suture, the surgeon begins closure at the caudal portion near the arcuate line, where the posterior sheath comes together easily due to minimal diastasis below the umbilicus.

The surgeon encounters increased tension while suturing the posterior sheath at the cephalad apex. The posterior sheath is freed up slightly to enhance mobility and facilitate medialization, ensuring the sutures hold without tearing the strong tissue layer.
The surgeon begins suturing at the cephalad portion using a 2-0, 180 V-lock, 9-inch suture, incorporating a section of barred soft mesh previously used for Morgagnia hernia repair.

The surgeon uses a small bite, small travel technique to re-approximate the posterior sheath in an area with significant tension. The posterior sheath is successfully brought together, excluding the peritoneal cavity from the surgical space.

A 17 cm by 27 cm piece of pritene mesh is cut into an oval shape and inserted through an 8 mm port. The surgeon uses a technique to roll a suture into the mesh roll, anchoring it halfway to prevent unfurling. The mesh is placed against the ipsilateral edge, and the surgeon adjusts its position slightly to the left. The mesh is held in place by friction without the use of drains, additional sutures, or fibrin sealant.
